original_txt:
China reports zero new Covid19 cases for FIRST TIME as pandemic finds new epicenter in South AmericaBeijing has reported no new Covid19 infections for the first time since the pandemic erupted last year but as the virus dies out in its original epicenter South America has quickly emerged as a major hotspot

Chinas National Health Commission said it confirmed zero new cases of the virus on Saturday  a first since the country began tallying infections in January  and reported only two suspected cases one of them imported from abroad

Good News Chinese mainland reports ZERO daily new COVID19 case 2 suspected cases reported  1 imported case in Shanghai and 1 domestic in NEChinas Jilin pictwittercom6SElh7UxUX  Global Times globaltimesnews May 23 2020

The lethal illness first emerged late last year in the Chinese city of Wuhan spreading across the country to infect some 84000 people killing in excess of 4600 But with aggressive containment measures that put tens of millions of citizens under strict lockdown Beijing appears to have largely brought the contagion under control reporting a only slow trickle of new cases as the virus took hold elsewhere in the world

While the United States remains the top epicenter with more than 16 million infections South America has quickly overtaken other regions for the numbertwo spot with the World Health Organization WHO warning of a dramatic spike in cases there

In a sense South America has become a new epicenter for the disease Dr Mike Ryan the WHOs top emergencies expert told a press conference on Friday noting that Brazil is clearly the most affected

Also on rtcom Still long way to go WHO records BIGGEST singleday increase in new Covid19 cases

Over the last week in Brazil health officials have reported tens of thousands of new infections each day bringing its current disease toll to more than 330000 rapidly eclipsing Russia as the secondworstaffected nation Brazil has also counted more than 21000 fatal cases nearly seven times more than Peru the regions next hardesthit country

Though no vaccine or even a reliable treatment currently exists for the coronavirus Brazilian authorities have approved widespread use of the antimalarial drug hydroxychloroquine as a therapy after receiving shipments of raw materials for the chemical from India last month The drug has shown early promise as a treatment but its effectiveness has yet to be proven in a clinical setting A new study in the Lancet journal meanwhile suggests the medicine could cause heart complications and even death showing little or no benefit to Covid19 patients

Since the pandemic broke out last December more than 52 million people have been sickened and some 338000 killed worldwide making it one of the deadliest viral outbreaks since the 1918 Spanish Flu

Also on rtcom Bolsonaro says experimental Covid19 drug could save thousands of lives  thanks India for approving exports of raw ingredients

Like this story Share it with a friend
corr_txt:
China reports zero new COVID-19 cases for the first time as pandemic shifts to South America.

Beijing has reported no new COVID-19 infections since the start of the outbreak last year, but as the virus wanes in its original epicenter, South America is quickly becoming a major hotspot.

The Chinese National Health Commission confirmed zero new cases on Saturday, marking the first such occurrence since January. The commission also noted two suspected cases, one imported from abroad and another domestic case in northeastern China's Jilin province.

Good News: Chinese mainland reports ZERO daily new COVID-19 case; 2 suspected cases reported (1 imported in Shanghai, 1 domestic in NE China’s Jilin) #GlobalTimes

The deadly illness first emerged late last year in Wuhan, spreading across the country to infect over 84,000 people and causing more than 4,600 deaths. However, with stringent containment measures that included strict lockdowns for tens of millions of citizens, Beijing appears to have largely brought the virus under control.

While the United States remains the top epicenter with over 16 million infections, South America has quickly surpassed other regions to become the second-most affected area. The World Health Organization (WHO) warns of a dramatic surge in cases there.

"South America is now a new epicenter for the disease," Dr. Mike Ryan, the WHO's top emergencies expert, said at a press conference on Friday, noting that Brazil is clearly the most impacted country.

Over the past week, Brazilian health officials have reported tens of thousands of new infections daily, bringing its total to more than 330,000 cases and surpassing Russia as the second-worst affected nation. The country has also recorded over 21,000 deaths, nearly seven times more than Peru, which is the next hardest-hit South American country.

Despite a lack of vaccine or reliable treatment for the coronavirus, Brazilian authorities have approved widespread use of hydroxychloroquine after receiving shipments of raw materials from India. While the drug has shown early promise as a potential therapy, its effectiveness remains unproven in clinical settings. A recent study published in The Lancet suggests that it could cause heart complications and even death with little to no benefit for COVID-19 patients.

Since December, more than 52 million people have been infected worldwide, resulting in over 338,000 deaths, making this one of the deadliest viral outbreaks since the 1918 Spanish Flu.
